votes.reason: The Python code using set equality correctly implements the Coq specification that two strings must contain the same set of characters.
The Coq specification requires that the set of characters in both strings be identical, which is exactly what the Python code checks using set(s0) == set(s1).
The Python code correctly uses set equality to check if both strings contain exactly the same characters, matching the Coq specification.

The Coq spec states b=true iff every character in s0 is in s1 and vice versa, which is exactly set equality. The Python code checks b == (set(s0) == set(s1)), which produces the same concrete result for all inputs.

The Coq spec states b = true iff every character in s0 is in s1 and vice versa, which is exactly set equality. The Python code checks b == (set(s0) == set(s1)), which produces the same concrete result for all inputs.

The Coq spec states b = true iff every character in s0 is in s1 and vice versa, which is exactly set equality. The Python code checks b == (set(s0) == set(s1)), which computes the same condition. Both return the same concrete result for any inputs.
Both check that b matches whether the two strings contain exactly the same set of characters (ignoring multiplicity), so the Python boolean equality mirrors the Coq iff condition.
Both check that b is true exactly when the two strings have the same set of characters (ignoring multiplicity); Python’s set(s0) == set(s1) matches the Coq membership equivalence.
Both define b to be true exactly when the two strings have the same set of characters (membership equivalence for all chars), ignoring order and duplicates.
